question_title: Will the IAEA conduct an on-site inspection at any of Iran's Natanz, Fordow, or Isfahan nuclear sites before September 1, 2026?
summary: Question requires a NEW IAEA on-site inspection of one of three specific bombed sites (Natanz, Fordow, or Isfahan) between July 1 and Aug 31, 2026. As of July 1-2, Iran legally bars IAEA from all three via parliamentary law + SNSC resolution (Qalibaf: 'no access whatsoever will be granted to bombed/damaged sites'); access permitted only to Bushehr and Tehran Research Reactor, neither of which counts. There is genuine, fast-moving diplomacy (Islamabad MoU in force with IAEA-supervision language, $6bn frozen funds releasing, 60-day oil waiver, positive Doha talks) and Grossi says inspections 'are going to happen' — but with no timeline. Iran explicitly conditions bombed-site access on a completed final deal + full sanctions relief, a high bar for a 2-month window, and these three sites are the most politically contested (they document strike damage). The status-quo legal ban dominates; diplomatic upside is real but unlikely to deliver a bombed-site inspection specifically before Sept 1.

factors.description: Status quo: Iran legally bars IAEA from all three bombed sites via parliamentary law + SNSC resolution, restated firmly July 1-2, 2026; access permitted only to Bushehr + Tehran Research Reactor (neither counts).
Active 60-day diplomatic window (Islamabad MoU in force, IAEA-supervision language, sanctions relief flowing, Doha talks 'going well') provides a real path to access.
Grossi says inspections 'going to happen' under the framework deal — but an expectation without a scheduled date.

The three sites are the most politically contested; Iran conditions access on a completed final deal + full sanctions relief, unlikely within 2 months, and any first inspections would target undamaged sites.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 15%**

The question requires a new IAEA on-site inspection of one of the three *specifically bombed* sites (Natanz, Fordow, or Isfahan) between July 1 and August 31, 2026.

Key reasoning:
- **Status quo strongly favors NO**: As of July 1-2, 2026, Iran legally bars IAEA from all three bombed sites via parliamentary law and an SNSC resolution. Parliament Speaker Qalibaf restated firmly: "no access whatsoever will be granted to sites that have been bombed and damaged." IAEA access is currently limited to Bushehr and the Tehran Research Reactor — neither of which counts for resolution.
- **Real upside from diplomacy**: The Islamabad MoU is in force with explicit IAEA-supervision language, sanctions relief is flowing ($6bn frozen funds, 60-day oil waiver), Doha talks are described as "going well," and Grossi says inspections "are going to happen." The resolution bar is low — a single managed visit to any one site would suffice.
- **But the hardest possible ask**: These three sites are the most politically symbolic (they document strike damage), and Iran explicitly conditions bombed-site access on a *completed* final deal with full sanctions relief — an unlikely outcome within the ~8-week window. Grossi's optimism carries no timeline, and breakthroughs of this magnitude typically slip.

The firm, recently-restated legal ban dominates, leaving genuine but limited upside from the active negotiations.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves as **Yes** if, after June 30, 2026 and before September 1, 2026, according to [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) conducts any type of on-site [inspection](https://www.iaea.org/zh/node/10022) of one or more of these Iranian nuclear sites: the Fordow Uranium Enrichment Plant, the Natanz Nuclear Facility, or  the Isfahan Nuclear Technology/Research Center.
fine_print: This question's information (resolution criteria, fine print, background info, etc) is synced with an [original identical question](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/44230) which opened on 2026-06-26 12:00:00. This question will resolve based on the resolution criteria and fine print of the linked original question. However, if this question would resolve differently than the original question, then this question will be annulled. Additionally, if the original question's resolution could have been known before this question opened, then this question will be annulled.

factors.description: Status quo: As of July 1-2, 2026, Iran legally bars IAEA from all three bombed sites via parliamentary law + SNSC resolution; Qalibaf: 'no access whatsoever will be granted to bombed/damaged sites.' Access permitted only to Bushehr + Tehran Research Reactor.

Active 60-day diplomatic window (Islamabad MoU in force June 17; ends ~mid-Aug) explicitly states nuclear activities 'will be supervised by IAEA'; sanctions relief flowing ($6bn, 60-day oil waiver); Doha talks 'going well.' Provides real upside path.
Grossi (June 24) says inspections 'going to happen' under framework deal — but no timeline, an expectation not a scheduled event.

The three sites are the MOST politically contested (symbolic damage documentation); Iran explicitly conditions bombed-site access on a FINAL deal + full sanctions relief, unlikely to fully complete within 2 months. Any first inspections would target undamaged sites first.

input.anchor: Contested, legally-banned access granted within a 2-month window during active-but-incomplete negotiations: roughly 15-20% base rate.
input.assessment: Question requires a NEW on-site inspection of one of the three bombed sites (Natanz, Fordow, Isfahan) between July 1 and Aug 31, 2026. Status quo strongly favors NO: Iran has a legal ban specifically on these sites, restated firmly July 1-2. Any inspection is a low bar (single visit to one site counts), and there is genuine, fast-moving diplomacy (MoU with IAEA supervision language, sanctions relief, positive Doha talks). But Iran explicitly ties bombed-site access to a completed final deal with full sanctions relief — a high bar for 2 months. Even Grossi's optimism lacks a timeline. Diplomatic breakthroughs of this magnitude typically slip. The specific requirement (bombed sites, not just any Iranian facility) is the hardest possible ask. I land ~15%: meaningful upside from active diplomacy, but dominated by Iran's firm legal ban and the short window.
input.key_uncertainties: Whether a final US-Iran deal is signed before mid-August and whether it would include a token inspection of even one bombed site (e.g., part of the large Isfahan complex) before Sept 1.
input.tool_audit: research() sub-agent returned rich, well-sourced, current findings from multiple credible outlets (Reuters/AP/Al Jazeera/CNN/ISIS). No failures. Sufficient to forecast without further tools.
input.process_reflection: The research tool worked excellently for this fast-moving geopolitical question, returning a clean timeline and current status with data points. No friction. A prediction-market check would have been nice for cross-validation but the situation is too niche/recent for liquid markets.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
All four factors are grounded in the research trace, with no hallucinations, no wrong-direction signs, and appropriate resolution alignment.

FACTOR CHECKS:
- F1 (status quo legal ban, -1.8): Verbatim support. Research key_facts confirm parliamentary law + SNSC resolution barring all three bombed sites; Qalibaf quote "no access whatsoever will be granted to sites that have been bombed and damaged" appears verbatim; access limited to Bushehr + Tehran Research Reactor (neither is a named site). Correctly handled.
- F2 (60-day diplomatic window, +1.0): Grounded — MoU with "supervised by the IAEA" language, $6bn frozen funds (data_point), 60-day oil waiver, Doha talks "going well" (CNN July 1). Minor date wobble (June 17 vs June 18 MoU entry-into-force across sources) is trivial and not forecast-affecting.
- F3 (Grossi "going to happen", +0.4): Grounded, June 24 Euronews/Al Jazeera, correctly characterized as expectation not scheduled event.
- F4 (bombed sites most contested, access tied to final deal, -0.9): Grounded — research states "bombed-site access is conditional on a final deal and full sanctions relief."

DOUBLE-COUNTING: Mild overlap between F1 and F4 (both reflect Iran's refusal) but they capture distinct mechanisms (current legal ban vs. forward-looking final-deal condition). F2/F3 both draw on the diplomacy story but are distinct signals. Not egregious.

RESOLUTION ALIGNMENT: Strong. The agent correctly targets the specific requirement (one of the three named bombed sites, not any Iranian facility) and the July 1–Aug 31 window. It correctly recognizes Bushehr/Tehran Research Reactor access does NOT help YES.

PRE-PUBLICATION EVENT: Window opens June 30, question opened July 3. Fine print annuls if resolution could have been known before opening. Research confirms zero inspections of the three sites as of July 1-2, so no already-resolved event exists — Case 2 clean.

ANCHOR: 15-20% anchor, 15% final — low edge but within range, and F1 (|logit|=1.8) is a genuine strong driver, so the placement is evidence-driven not narrative-driven.

COUNTERARGUMENT: Strong and genuine — builds a coherent alternative (fast-moving diplomacy already in force, low resolution bar of a single managed visit, Grossi's institutional authority, Isfahan's sprawling complex). Not a token gesture.

CONFIDENCE (0.70): Calibrated for genuine two-sided uncertainty.

MINOR NOTE (not forecast-affecting): The tool_audit narrative says "No failures," but ground-truth metrics show 4 errors/35 calls (fetch_url 100% error, execute_code 14.3%). However, the research() tool itself returned 0 failures and none of the failed calls degraded the evidence base, so the substantive claim about research quality holds.

INDEPENDENT ESTIMATE: Firm recently-restated legal ban on exactly these three sites, conditioned on a final deal unlikely to close within ~8 weeks, weighed against real but incomplete diplomatic momentum and a low resolution bar. I land ~12-18%, consistent with the agent's 15%. No error affecting forecast direction or magnitude.
